title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Evening Shift
question: Let $z=(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i) \ldots .(1+n i)$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$. If $|z|^2=44200$, then $n$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & Z=(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i) \ldots(1+n i) \\ & |Z|=|(1+i)(1+2 i)(1+3 i) \ldots(1+n i)| \\ & |Z|=|1+i| \cdot|1+2 i| \cdot|1+3 i| \ldots|1+n i| \\ & |Z|^2=|1+i|^2 \cdot|1+2 i|^2 \cdot|1+3 i|^2 \ldots|1+n i|^2 \\ & |Z|^2=\left(1^2+1^2\right)\left(1^2+2^2\right)\left(1^2+3^2\right) \ldots\left(1^2+n^2\right)\left\{\text { as } z=x+i y,|z|^2=x^2+y^2\right\} \end{aligned} $$

It is given that

$$ \begin{aligned} & |Z|^2=44200 \\ & \left(1+1^2\right)\left(1+2^2\right)\left(1+3^2\right) \ldots\left(1+n^2\right)=44200 \\ & 44200=2^3 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 13 \cdot 17 \\ & \left(1+1^2\right)\left(1+2^2\right)\left(1+3^2\right) \cdot\left(1+4^2\right)\left(1+5^2\right) \ldots\left(1+n^2\right)=2^3 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 13 \cdot 17 \end{aligned} $$

solve some product terms

$$ 2 \cdot 5 \cdot(10) \cdot(17) \cdot(26) \ldots\left(1+n^2\right)=(2)(5)(2 \times 5)(17)(2 \times 13) $$

this matches for $n=5$

so value of $n=5$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 24th January Morning Shift
question:
Let $\mathrm{S}=\left\{z \in \mathbb{C}:\left|\frac{z-6 i}{z-2 i}\right|=1\right.$ and $\left.\left|\frac{z-8+2 i}{z+2 i}\right|=\frac{3}{5}\right\}$.

Then $\sum\limits_{z \in \mathrm{~s}}|z|^2$ is equal to :
solution:
Given, $\mathrm{S}=\left\{z \in \mathbb{C}:\left|\frac{z-6 i}{z-2 i}\right|=1\right.$ and $\left.\left|\frac{z-8+2 i}{z+2 i}\right|=\frac{3}{5}\right\}$

$\left|\frac{z-6 i}{z-2 i}\right|=1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ |z-6 i|=|z-2 i|-(1) $$

equation (1) represent $z$ is a point which is equidistant from point $(0,2)$ and $(0,6)$ so $z$ lies on the line segment which is perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining point $(0,2)$ & $ (0,6)$.

If $z=x+i y$ then solution of equation (1) is $y=\frac{6+2}{2}=4$ and $x \in R$.

So, solution is $z=x+4 i$ & $ x \in R$.

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Solving }\left|\frac{z-8+2 i}{z+2 i}\right|=\frac{3}{5} \\\\ & \text { Substitute } z=x+4 i \\\\ & \frac{|x+4 i-8+2 i|}{|x+4 i+2 i|}=\frac{3}{5}\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $5|x+6 i-8|=3|x+6 i|$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $5|(x-8)+6 i|=3|x+6 i|$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $5 \sqrt{(x-8)^2+6^2}=3 \sqrt{x^2+6^2}$

Squaring

$$\Rightarrow $$ $ 25\left(x^2+64-16 x+36\right)=9 x^2+324$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $25 x^2-400 x+2500=9 x^2+324$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $16 x^2-400 x+2176=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $x^2-25 x+136=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $x^2-17 x-8 x+136=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $x(x-17)-8(x-17)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(x-17)(x-8)=0$

$$\therefore $$ $x=17, x=8$

So set S contain two complex number $z_1=17+4 i$ and $z_2=8+4 i$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { To find } \sum_{z \in S}|z|^2=\left|z_1\right|^2+\left|z_2\right|^2 \\\\ & =|17+4 i|^2+|8+4 i|^2 \\\\ & =17^2+4^2+8^2+4^2 \\\\ & =289+16+64+16=385\end{aligned}$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Evening Shift
question:
Let

$A = \{ z \in \mathbb{C} : |z - 2| \leq 4 \}$ and

$B = \{ z \in \mathbb{C} : |z - 2| + |z + 2| = 5 \}$.

Then the max $\{|z_1 - z_2| : z_1 \in A \text{ and } z_2 \in B \}$ is :
solution:
Analyze Set $A$ :

The set $A$ is defined as $\{z \in \mathbb{C}:|z-2| \leq 4\}$.

  • This represents a circle (and its interior) centered at $(2,0)$ with a radius $r=4$.

  • The circle extends from $x=-2$ to $x=6$ along the real axis.

Analyze Set $B$ :

The set $B$ is defined as $\{z \in \mathbb{C}:|z-2|+|z+2|=5\}$.

  • By the definition of an ellipse, the sum of distances from two fixed points (foci) is constant.

  • Here, the foci are $F_1(2,0)$ and $F_2(-2,0)$, and the length of the major axis $2 a=5$, so $a=2.5$.

  • By the definition of an ellipse, the sum of distances from two fixed points (foci) is constant.

  • Here, the foci are $F_1(2,0)$ and $F_2(-2,0)$, and the length of the major axis $2 a=5$, so $a=2.5$.

  • The distance between foci is $2 a e=4$, so $e=\frac{4}{5}=0.8$.

  • The semi-minor axis $b$ can be found using $b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right)=(2.5)^2(1-0.64)= 6.25(0.36)=2.25$, so $b=1.5$.

  • The ellipse is centered at the origin $(0,0)$ and extends from $x=-2.5$ to $x=2.5$ along the real axis.

To find $\max \left\{\left|z_1-z_2\right|: z_1 \in A\right.$ and $\left.z_2 \in B\right\} \quad$ This means maximize the distance between a point $z_1$ in the circle and a point $z_2$ on the ellipse.

The furthest point in set $A$ (the circle) is at the rightmost edge, $z_1=(2+4,0)=(6,0)$.

The furthest point in set $B$ (the ellipse) from $z_1$ is at its leftmost edge, $z_2=(-2.5,0)$.

Maximum Distance $\left|z_1-z_2\right|=|6-(-2.5)|=|6+2.5|=8.5=\frac{17}{2}$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift
question: Let $z$ be a complex number such that $|z-6|=5$ and $|z+2-6 i|=5$. Then the value of $z^3+3 z^2-15 z+141$ is equal to :
solution:
Let $z=x+i y$

$|z-(\alpha+i \beta)|=r$ represents a circle in the complex plane with centre at $(\alpha, \beta)$ and radius is $r$ unit.

Cartesian equation

$$ (x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=r^2 $$

The equations $|z-6|=5$ and $|z-(-2+6 i)|=5$ describe two circles with the same radius $(r=5)$.

  • Circle 1 : Center $C_1=(6,0)$

  • Circle 2 : Center $C_2=(-2,6)$

Cartesian equation

$$ (x-6)^2+y^2=25 \quad \text { and } \quad(x+2)^2+(y-6)^2=25 $$

Expanding both:

  • $x^2-12 x+36+y^2=25 \Longrightarrow \mathbf{x}^2+\mathbf{y}^2-12 \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{1 1}=\mathbf{0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

  • $x^2+4 x+4+y^2-12 y+36=25 \Longrightarrow \mathbf{x}^2+\mathbf{y}^2+\mathbf{4} \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{1 2} \mathbf{y}+\mathbf{1 5}=\mathbf{0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$

Subtracting the first equation from the second to find the common chord:

$$ \begin{gathered} (4 x-(-12 x))-12 y+(15-11)=0 \\ 16 x-12 y+4=0 \Longrightarrow \mathbf{4}-\mathbf{3} \mathbf{y}+\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{0} \Longrightarrow \mathbf{y}=\frac{\mathbf{4} \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{3}} \end{gathered} $$

Substitute $y$ back into the first circle equation:

$$ (x-6)^2+\left(\frac{4 x+1}{3}\right)^2=25 $$

multiply both sides by 9

$$ \begin{aligned} & 9\left(x^2+36-12 x\right)+16 x^2+8 x+1=225 \\ & 25 x^2-100 x+100=0 \\ & x^2-4 x+4=0 \\ & (x-2)^2=0 \Rightarrow x=2 \end{aligned} $$

put $x=2$ in $y=(4 x+1) / 3$

$$ y=(4 \times 2+1) / 3=9 / 3=3 $$

$$ \text { so } z=2+3 i $$

calculate $z^3+3 z^2-15 z+141$

Compute $z^3$

$$ \begin{gathered} z^3=z \cdot z^2=(2+3 i)(-5+12 i) \\ =-10+24 i-15 i+36 i^2 \\ =-10+9 i-36 \\ =-46+9 i \end{gathered} $$

Compute $z^2$

$$ (2+3 i)^2=4+12 i+9 i^2=4+12 i-9=-5+12 i $$

so

$$ \begin{gathered} z^3=-46+9 i \\ 3 z^2=-15+36 i \\ -15 z=-30-45 i \end{gathered} $$

Add everything

$$ (-46+9 i)+(-15+36 i)+(-30-45 i)+141 $$

Real parts:

$$ -46-15-30+141=50 $$

Imaginary parts:

$$ 9 i+36 i-45 i=0 $$

So $z^3+3 z^2-15 z+141=50$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Evening Shift
question:
Let the circles $C_1:|z| = r$ and $C_2:|z - 3 - 4i| = 5$, $z \in \mathbb{C}$, be such that $C_2$ lies within $C_1$.
If $z_1$ moves on $C_1$, $z_2$ moves on $C_2$ and $\min |z_1 - z_2| = 2$, then $\max |z_1 - z_2|$ is equal to :
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & |z|=r \\ & O A=r \end{aligned} $$

Step 1: Circle $C_1:|z|=r$ has centre $O(0,0)$ and radius $r$ (so $OA=r$).

Step 2: Circle $C_2:|z-3-4i|=5$ has centre at the point $(3,4)$ and radius $5$.

Step 3: Distance between the centres is

$$OC=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5$$

Step 4: Since $C_2$ lies completely inside $C_1$, the minimum distance between a point $z_1$ on $C_1$ and a point $z_2$ on $C_2$ happens when the two points are on the same line through the centres, on the nearest sides.

So,

minimum $\left|\mathrm{z}_1-\mathrm{z}_2\right|= r-(OC+5)=r-(5+5)=r-10$

Given minimum $\left|\mathrm{z}_1-\mathrm{z}_2\right|=2$, we get

$$ \Rightarrow r-10=2 \Rightarrow r=12 $$

Step 5: The maximum distance $\left|z_1-z_2\right|$ happens when $z_1$ and $z_2$ are on opposite sides along the same line through the centres.

Then the farthest distance is

$$ \left|z_1-z_2\right|= r+(OC+5)=r+10 $$

Substituting $r=12$,

$$ \left|z_1-z_2\right|=12+10=22 $$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 2nd April Morning Shift
question: Let $x$ and $y$ be real numbers such that $50\left(\frac{2 x}{1+3 i}-\frac{y}{1-2 i}\right)=31+17 i, i=\sqrt{-1}$. Then the value of $10(x-3 y)$ is :
solution:
$ 50\left(\frac{2x}{1+3i}-\frac{y}{1-2i}\right)=31+17i $

We need to find $10(x-3y)$.

First, simplify the fractions by rationalising the denominators.

$ \frac{2x}{1+3i}=\frac{2x(1-3i)}{(1+3i)(1-3i)} $

$ =\frac{2x(1-3i)}{1+9}=\frac{2x(1-3i)}{10} $

$ =\frac{x}{5}(1-3i) $

Similarly,

$ \frac{y}{1-2i}=\frac{y(1+2i)}{(1-2i)(1+2i)} $

$ =\frac{y(1+2i)}{1+4}=\frac{y(1+2i)}{5} $

Now substitute into the given equation:

$ 50\left(\frac{x}{5}(1-3i)-\frac{y}{5}(1+2i)\right)=31+17i $

Take $\frac{1}{5}$ common inside:

$ 50\cdot \frac{1}{5}\left[x(1-3i)-y(1+2i)\right]=31+17i $

$ 10\left[x(1-3i)-y(1+2i)\right]=31+17i $

Expand:

$ 10\left[x-3xi-y-2yi\right]=31+17i $

$ 10\left[(x-y)+(-3x-2y)i\right]=31+17i $

So,

$ 10(x-y)+10(-3x-2y)i=31+17i $

Now compare real and imaginary parts:

$ 10(x-y)=31 $

$ 10(-3x-2y)=17 $

Thus,

$ x-y=\frac{31}{10} $

$ -3x-2y=\frac{17}{10} $

Now solve these equations.

From the first equation,

$ x=y+\frac{31}{10} $

Substitute into the second:

$ -3\left(y+\frac{31}{10}\right)-2y=\frac{17}{10} $

$ -3y-\frac{93}{10}-2y=\frac{17}{10} $

$ -5y=\frac{17}{10}+\frac{93}{10} $

$ -5y=\frac{110}{10}=11 $

$ y=-\frac{11}{5} $

Then

$ x=y+\frac{31}{10}=-\frac{11}{5}+\frac{31}{10} $

$ x=-\frac{22}{10}+\frac{31}{10}=\frac{9}{10} $

Now find:

$ 10(x-3y)=10\left(\frac{9}{10}-3\left(-\frac{11}{5}\right)\right) $

$ =10\left(\frac{9}{10}+\frac{33}{5}\right) $

$ =10\left(\frac{9}{10}+\frac{66}{10}\right) $

$ =10\cdot \frac{75}{10}=75 $

Therefore, the value of $10(x-3y)$ is

$ \boxed{75} $

So, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Evening Shift
question: Let $\mathrm{S}=\left\{z \in \mathrm{C}: z^2+4 z+16=0\right\}$. Then $\sum\limits_{z \in \mathrm{~S}}|z+\sqrt{3} \mathrm{i}|^2$ is equal to :
solution:
Solve the quadratic equation first:

$$ z^2+4z+16=0 $$

Using the quadratic formula,

$$ z=\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{16-64}}{2} =\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{-48}}{2} =\frac{-4\pm 4\sqrt{3}i}{2} =-2\pm 2\sqrt{3}i $$

So the set is

$$ S=\{-2+2\sqrt{3}i,\,-2-2\sqrt{3}i\} $$

Now we need to find

$$ \sum_{z\in S}|z+\sqrt{3}i|^2 $$

That is,

$$ |-2+2\sqrt{3}i+\sqrt{3}i|^2 + |-2-2\sqrt{3}i+\sqrt{3}i|^2 $$

Simplify each term:

  1. For $z=-2+2\sqrt{3}i$,

$$ z+\sqrt{3}i=-2+3\sqrt{3}i $$

Hence,

$$ |-2+3\sqrt{3}i|^2=(-2)^2+(3\sqrt{3})^2=4+27=31 $$

  1. For $z=-2-2\sqrt{3}i$,

$$ z+\sqrt{3}i=-2-\sqrt{3}i $$

Hence,

$$ |-2-\sqrt{3}i|^2=(-2)^2+(-\sqrt{3})^2=4+3=7 $$

Therefore,

$$ \sum_{z\in S}|z+\sqrt{3}i|^2=31+7=38 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{38} $$

Option D

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift
question: Let $z$ be a complex number such that $|z+2|=|z-2|$ and arg $\left(\frac{z+3}{z-i}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}$. Then $|z|^2$ is equal to:
solution:
Let

$$ z = x + iy $$

where $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$.

We are given:

$$ |z+2| = |z-2| $$

and

$$ \arg\left(\frac{z+3}{z-i}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4} $$

We will use these one by one.

1. Using $|z+2| = |z-2|$

Substitute $z = x+iy$:

$$ |x+iy+2| = |x+iy-2| $$

So,

$$ \sqrt{(x+2)^2+y^2} = \sqrt{(x-2)^2+y^2} $$

Squaring both sides:

$$ (x+2)^2 + y^2 = (x-2)^2 + y^2 $$

$$ x^2+4x+4 = x^2-4x+4 $$

$$ 8x = 0 $$

$$ x = 0 $$

Hence,

$$ z = iy $$

So the point lies on the imaginary axis.

2. Using the argument condition

Now,

$$ \frac{z+3}{z-i} = \frac{3+iy}{i(y-1)} $$

We are given that its argument is $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

A complex number has argument $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ when its real and imaginary parts are equal and positive in sign together.

Let us simplify:

$$ \frac{3+iy}{i(y-1)} $$

Since $\dfrac{1}{i} = -i$, we get

$$ \frac{3+iy}{i(y-1)} = \frac{-i(3+iy)}{y-1} $$

Now expand:

$$ -i(3+iy) = -3i - i^2y = y - 3i $$

So,

$$ \frac{z+3}{z-i} = \frac{y-3i}{y-1} = \frac{y}{y-1} - i\frac{3}{y-1} $$

Thus real part is

$$ \frac{y}{y-1} $$

and imaginary part is

$$ -\frac{3}{y-1} $$

Since argument is $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, we need

$$ \frac{\text{imaginary part}}{\text{real part}} = \tan\frac{\pi}{4} = 1 $$

So,

$$ \frac{-\frac{3}{y-1}}{\frac{y}{y-1}} = 1 $$

$$ -\frac{3}{y} = 1 $$

$$ y = -3 $$

Therefore,

$$ z = -3i $$

3. Find $|z|^2$

$$ |z|^2 = 0^2 + (-3)^2 = 9 $$

So,

$$ \boxed{9} $$

Hence, the correct option is Option A.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Evening Shift
question:
Let $z_1, z_2 \in \mathbb{C}$ be the distinct solutions of the equation $z^2+4 z-(1+12 i)=0$.

Then $\left|z_1\right|^2+\left|z_2\right|^2$ is equal to :
solution:
Given the quadratic equation

$$ z^2+4z-(1+12i)=0 $$

let its roots be $z_1$ and $z_2$.

We need to find

$$ |z_1|^2+|z_2|^2 $$

Using Vieta’s formulas for

$$ az^2+bz+c=0, $$

we have

$$ z_1+z_2=-\frac{b}{a}=-4 $$

and

$$ z_1z_2=\frac{c}{a}=-(1+12i)=-1-12i $$

Now solve the equation to identify the roots.

$$ z^2+4z-(1+12i)=0 $$

Using the quadratic formula,

$$ z=\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{16+4(1+12i)}}{2} $$

$$ z=\frac{-4\pm\sqrt{20+48i}}{2} $$

Now observe that

$$ (6+4i)^2=36+48i-16=20+48i $$

So,

$$ \sqrt{20+48i}=6+4i $$

Hence the roots are

$$ z_1=\frac{-4+(6+4i)}{2}=1+2i $$

and

$$ z_2=\frac{-4-(6+4i)}{2}=-5-2i $$

Now compute their moduli squared:

$$ |z_1|^2=|1+2i|^2=1^2+2^2=5 $$

$$ |z_2|^2=|-5-2i|^2=(-5)^2+(-2)^2=25+4=29 $$

Therefore,

$$ |z_1|^2+|z_2|^2=5+29=34 $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{34} $$

Hence, Option D is correct.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 5th April Morning Shift
question: Let $a, b \in \mathbb{C}$. Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the equation $x^2+a x+b=0$. If $\beta-\alpha=\sqrt{11}$ and $\beta^2-\alpha^2=3 i \sqrt{11}$, then $\left(\beta^3-\alpha^3\right)^2$ is equal to:
solution:
Given:

  • $\alpha,\beta$ are roots of $x^2+ax+b=0$

  • $\beta-\alpha=\sqrt{11}$

  • $\beta^2-\alpha^2=3i\sqrt{11}$

We have to find $\left(\beta^3-\alpha^3\right)^2$.

Now,

$$ \beta^2-\alpha^2=(\beta-\alpha)(\beta+\alpha) $$

Substituting the given values,

$$ 3i\sqrt{11}=\sqrt{11}(\beta+\alpha) $$

Since $\sqrt{11}\neq 0$, we get

$$ \beta+\alpha=3i $$

Now use the identity

$$ \beta^3-\alpha^3=(\beta-\alpha)(\beta^2+\alpha\beta+\alpha^2) $$

So first let us find $\beta^2+\alpha\beta+\alpha^2$.

We know

$$ (\beta+\alpha)^2=\beta^2+\alpha^2+2\alpha\beta $$

Also,

$$ \alpha\beta=\frac{(\alpha+\beta)^2-(\beta-\alpha)^2}{4} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ \alpha+\beta=3i,\qquad \beta-\alpha=\sqrt{11} $$

Thus,

$$ \alpha\beta=\frac{(3i)^2-(\sqrt{11})^2}{4} =\frac{-9-11}{4} =\frac{-20}{4} =-5 $$

Now,

$$ \alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha\beta $$

So,

$$ \alpha^2+\beta^2=(3i)^2-2(-5)=-9+10=1 $$

Hence,

$$ \beta^2+\alpha\beta+\alpha^2=1+(-5)=-4 $$

Therefore,

$$ \beta^3-\alpha^3=(\beta-\alpha)(\beta^2+\alpha\beta+\alpha^2) =\sqrt{11}\cdot(-4) =-4\sqrt{11} $$

Now square it:

$$ \left(\beta^3-\alpha^3\right)^2=\left(-4\sqrt{11}\right)^2=16\cdot 11=176 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{176} $$

Hence, Option B is correct.

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift
question: Let $S=\left\{z \in \mathbb{C}: z^2+\sqrt{6} i z-3=0\right\}$. Then $\sum\limits_{z \in S} z^8$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & z=\frac{-\sqrt{6} i \pm \sqrt{-6+12}}{2} \\ & =\frac{-\sqrt{6} i \pm \sqrt{6}}{2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}( \pm 1-i)\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \alpha=\frac{-\sqrt{6}}{2}(1+\mathrm{i}), \beta=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}(1-\mathrm{i}) \\ & \alpha=\frac{-\sqrt{6}}{2} \times \sqrt{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \pi / 4}, \beta=+\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} \times \sqrt{2}-\mathrm{i} \pi / 4 \\ & \alpha=-\sqrt{3} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \pi / 4}, \beta=+\sqrt{3} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i} \pi / 4}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \alpha^8+\beta^8=81 \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} 2 \pi}+81 \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}(-2 \pi)} \\ & =81(1+1)=162\end{aligned}$

title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift
question: Let the set of all values of $k \in \mathbb{R}$ such that the equation $z(\bar{z}+2+i)+k(2+3 i)=0, z \in \mathrm{C}$, has at least one solution, be the interval $[\alpha, \beta]$. Then $9(\alpha+\beta)$ is equal to:
solution:
Let

$$ z = x+iy \qquad \text{where } x,y \in \mathbb{R}. $$

Then

$$ \bar z = x-iy. $$

The given equation is

$$ z(\bar z+2+i)+k(2+3i)=0. $$

We have to find all real values of $k$ for which this equation has at least one complex solution $z$.

First, simplify $z(\bar z+2+i)$:

$$ z(\bar z+2+i)=z\bar z+z(2+i). $$

Now,

$$ z\bar z = |z|^2 = x^2+y^2 $$

and

$$ z(2+i)=(x+iy)(2+i). $$

Let us multiply:

$$ (x+iy)(2+i)=2x+ix+2iy+i^2y $$

Since $i^2=-1$,

$$ (x+iy)(2+i)=(2x-y)+i(x+2y). $$

So,

$$ z(\bar z+2+i)=x^2+y^2+(2x-y)+i(x+2y). $$

Hence the given equation becomes

$$ \left(x^2+y^2+2x-y\right)+i(x+2y)+k(2+3i)=0. $$

Now separate real and imaginary parts:

$$ \left(x^2+y^2+2x-y+2k\right)+i\left(x+2y+3k\right)=0. $$

Therefore,

$$ x^2+y^2+2x-y+2k=0 \qquad \text{and} \qquad x+2y+3k=0. $$

From the second equation,

$$ 3k=-(x+2y) $$

so

$$ k=-\frac{x+2y}{3}. $$

Substitute this into the first equation:

$$ x^2+y^2+2x-y+2\left(-\frac{x+2y}{3}\right)=0. $$

Multiply by $3$:

$$ 3x^2+3y^2+6x-3y-2x-4y=0 $$

$$ 3x^2+3y^2+4x-7y=0. $$

Now complete squares:

$$ 3\left(x^2+\frac{4}{3}x\right)+3\left(y^2-\frac{7}{3}y\right)=0. $$

$$ 3\left[\left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2-\frac{4}{9}\right] +3\left[\left(y-\frac{7}{6}\right)^2-\frac{49}{36}\right]=0. $$

$$ 3\left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2-\frac{4}{3} +3\left(y-\frac{7}{6}\right)^2-\frac{49}{12}=0. $$

$$ 3\left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2+3\left(y-\frac{7}{6}\right)^2 =\frac{4}{3}+\frac{49}{12}. $$

$$ 3\left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2+3\left(y-\frac{7}{6}\right)^2 =\frac{16+49}{12}=\frac{65}{12}. $$

So,

$$ \left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2+\left(y-\frac{7}{6}\right)^2=\frac{65}{36}. $$

This is a circle with centre

$$ \left(-\frac{2}{3},\frac{7}{6}\right) $$

and radius

$$ \frac{\sqrt{65}}{6}. $$

Now,

$$ k=-\frac{x+2y}{3}. $$

So we need the range of the linear expression $x+2y$ on this circle.

Let

$$ S=x+2y. $$

For a circle, maximum and minimum of a linear expression occur at the extreme points in the direction of its normal vector.

At the centre,

$$ S_c=-\frac{2}{3}+2\cdot\frac{7}{6} =-\frac{2}{3}+\frac{7}{3} =\frac{5}{3}. $$

The vector corresponding to $x+2y$ is $(1,2)$, whose magnitude is

$$ \sqrt{1^2+2^2}=\sqrt{5}. $$

Hence the maximum variation from the centre is

$$ \text{radius} \times \sqrt{5} =\frac{\sqrt{65}}{6}\cdot\sqrt{5} =\frac{\sqrt{325}}{6} =\frac{5\sqrt{13}}{6}. $$

Therefore,

$$ S_{\min}=\frac{5}{3}-\frac{5\sqrt{13}}{6}, \qquad S_{\max}=\frac{5}{3}+\frac{5\sqrt{13}}{6}. $$

Since

$$ k=-\frac{S}{3}, $$

the interval for $k$ is

$$ \left[-\frac{S_{\max}}{3},-\frac{S_{\min}}{3}\right]. $$

So if this interval is $[\alpha,\beta]$, then

$$ \alpha+\beta = -\frac{S_{\max}+S_{\min}}{3} = -\frac{2\cdot \frac{5}{3}}{3} = -\frac{10}{9}. $$

Hence,

$$ 9(\alpha+\beta)=9\left(-\frac{10}{9}\right)=-10. $$

Therefore, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{-10}. $$

So, Option A is correct.

title: NDA Mathematics 10 April 2022
question: What is the principal argument of $\frac{1}{1 + i}$ where $i = \sqrt{-1}?$
solution:
To determine the principal argument of the complex number $$\frac{1}{1 + i}$$ where $$i = \sqrt{-1}$$, we will perform the following steps:

  1. Find the complex conjugate of the denominator.
  2. Multiply the numerator and the denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator.
  3. Simplify the expression to obtain the complex number in standard form $a + bi$.
  4. Calculate the argument (angle) of the resulting complex number.

Step 1: The complex conjugate of $1 + i$ is $1 - i$.

Step 2: Multiply the numerator and the denominator by the complex conjugate $1 - i$.

$$\frac{1}{1 + i} \cdot \frac{1 - i}{1 - i} = \frac{1 - i}{(1 + i)(1 - i)}$$

Step 3: Simplify the denominator:

$$(1 + i)(1 - i) = 1^2 - i^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

Thus,

$$\frac{1 - i}{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{i}{2}$$

Which means the complex number in standard form is $\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}i$.

Step 4: Calculate the argument:

The argument $\theta$ of a complex number $a + bi$ is given by:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

For $\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}i$, $a = \frac{1}{2}$ and $b = -\frac{1}{2}$.

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-1)$$

The value of $\tan^{-1}(-1)$ is $-\frac{\pi}{4}$, which is in the range $-\pi < \theta \leq \pi$ for the principal argument.

Therefore, the principal argument of $$\frac{1}{1 + i}$$ is:

Option B: $$-\frac{\pi}{4}$$

title: NDA Mathematics 10 April 2022
question: What is the modulus of $\left(\frac{\sqrt{-3}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{200}?$
solution:
To determine the modulus of the given complex number expression, we first need to identify the modulus of the base complex number before raising it to the $200$th power.

The given complex number is:

$$\left(\frac{\sqrt{-3}}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

We can rewrite this as:

$$\frac{\sqrt{-3}}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}i - 1}{2}$$

Let's denote the complex number as $z$:

$$z = \frac{\sqrt{3}i - 1}{2}$$

We need to find the modulus of $z$. The modulus of a complex number $a + bi$ is given by:

$$|a + bi| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$$

For our complex number $z = \frac{\sqrt{3}i - 1}{2}$, $a = -\frac{1}{2}$ and $b = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

Therefore, the modulus of $z$ is:

$$|z| = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}$$

Simplifying the expression inside the square root:

$$|z| = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

This shows that the modulus of the complex number $z = \frac{\sqrt{3}i - 1}{2}$ is $1$.

Now, we need to find the modulus of $z^{200}$. Since the modulus of a power of a complex number is the power of the modulus of that number, we get:

$$|z^{200}| = |z|^{200} = 1^{200} = 1$$

Therefore, the modulus of $\left(\frac{\sqrt{-3}}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{200}$ is $1$.

The correct answer is:

Option C: 1

title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
question: What is $ \left( \frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{\sqrt{3}-i} \right)^3 $ equal to?
solution:
We need to simplify

$ \left( \frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{\sqrt{3}-i} \right)^3 $

Step 1: Simplify the fraction inside.

$ \frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{\sqrt{3}-i} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{\sqrt{3}+i} = \frac{(\sqrt{3}+i)^2}{(\sqrt{3})^2 - (i)^2} $

Step 2: Simplify the denominator.

$ (\sqrt{3})^2 - (i)^2 = 3 - (-1) = 4 $

Step 3: Expand the numerator.

$ (\sqrt{3}+i)^2 = (\sqrt{3})^2 + 2\sqrt{3}i + i^2 = 3 + 2\sqrt{3}i - 1 = 2 + 2\sqrt{3}i $

So,

$ \frac{(\sqrt{3}+i)^2}{4} = \frac{2 + 2\sqrt{3}i}{4} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i $

Step 4: Recognize the form.

$ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i = \cos 60^\circ + i \sin 60^\circ $

So the fraction equals $e^{i\pi/3}$.

Step 5: Raise to the power 3.

$ \left(e^{i\pi/3}\right)^3 = e^{i\pi} = \cos \pi + i\sin \pi = -1 $

Final Answer:

Option A: -1

title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
question: If $z\ne0$ is a complex number, then what is amp(z)+amp(zˉ) equal to?
solution:
1. Let $z\neq0$ and write in polar form

    $ z = r\,e^{i\theta},\quad r>0,\ \theta=\arg(z). $

  2. Then its conjugate is

    $ \bar z = r\,e^{-i\theta},\quad \arg(\bar z) = -\theta. $

  3. Hence

    $ \arg(z) + \arg(\bar z) = \theta + (-\theta) = 0 \quad(\text{mod }2\pi), $

    and on the principal branch $\arg\in(-\pi,\pi]$ it equals $0$.

Answer: 0.

title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
question: If x<sup>2</sup> + x + 1 = 0, then what is the value of x<sup>199</sup> + x<sup>200</sup> + x<sup>201</sup>?
solution:
We start with the equation:

$$ x^2 + x + 1 = 0. $$

From this equation, one key observation is that its solutions are the non-real cube roots of unity. This means that if $ x $ is a solution, then

$$ x^3 = 1. $$

Now, let's simplify the expression $ x^{199} + x^{200} + x^{201} $ using the fact that the powers of $ x $ repeat every 3 steps. Here's how:

Express the exponents modulo 3:

$199 \mod 3 = 1$, so $ x^{199} = x^1 = x $.

$200 \mod 3 = 2$, so $ x^{200} = x^2 $.

$201 \mod 3 = 0$, so $ x^{201} = x^0 = 1 $.

Substitute these back into the expression:

$$ x^{199} + x^{200} + x^{201} = x + x^2 + 1. $$

Use the given equation:

Notice that the expression $ x + x^2 + 1 $ is exactly the left-hand side of the equation $ x^2 + x + 1 = 0 $. Therefore,

$$ x + x^2 + 1 = 0. $$

So, the value of $ x^{199} + x^{200} + x^{201} $ is 0.

Thus, the correct answer is Option B: 0.

title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
question: What is $\rm \sum\limits_{n=1}^{8n+7} i^n$ equal to, where i = √-1?
solution:
$\rm \sum\limits_{n=1}^{8n +7} i^n$

⇒ i1 + i2  + i3 + i4 + i5 + i6 + i7 + ..... + i8n+7 

⇒  i1 + i2  + i3 + i4 + i5 + i6 + i7 + ..... +  i8n +  i8n+1 + i8n+2 + .... +  i8n+6  + i8n+7  

⇒ (i -1 - i + 1) +  (i -1 - i + 1) + .... +  (i8n+1 + i8n+2 +  i8n+3 + i8n+4 ) +  (i8n+5 +  i8n+6  + i8n+7)

⇒ 0 + i8n+5 +  i8n+6  + i8n+7 = i1 + i2  + i3 =  i -1 - i = -1

∴ The value of  $\rm \sum\limits_{n=1}^{8n +7} i^n$ is - 1.

title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
question: If z = x + iy, where i = √-1, then what does the equations zz̅ + ∣z∣2 + 4(z + z̅) - 48 = 0 represent?
solution:
We are given:

$$ z = x + iy, \quad \text{where} \quad i = \sqrt{-1} $$

and the equation:

$$ z\bar{z} + |z|^2 + 4(z + \bar{z}) - 48 = 0. $$

Notice that:

The term $$ z\bar{z} $$ is the same as $$ |z|^2 $$, which is equal to $$ x^2 + y^2. $$

Also, $$ z + \bar{z} = (x+iy) + (x-iy) = 2x. $$

Substitute these into the equation:

$$ |z|^2 + |z|^2 + 4(2x) - 48 = 0 $$

$$ \Rightarrow 2(x^2 + y^2) + 8x - 48 = 0. $$

Divide the entire equation by 2 to simplify:

$$ x^2 + y^2 + 4x - 24 = 0. $$

Complete the square for the $x$-terms:

Write $ x^2 + 4x $ as $ (x^2 + 4x + 4) - 4 $.

The equation becomes:

$$ (x^2 + 4x + 4) + y^2 - 24 - 4 = 0 $$

$$ \Rightarrow (x + 2)^2 + y^2 = 28. $$

The equation

$$ (x + 2)^2 + y^2 = 28 $$

represents a circle with:

Center at $ (-2, 0) $

Radius $ \sqrt{28} $ or $ 2\sqrt{7} $.

Thus, the given equation represents a circle.

The correct answer is: Option C (Circle).

title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
question: Which one of the following is a square root of $\rm 2a+2\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$, where a, b ∈ ℝ?
solution:
Let's verify Option A by checking whether its square equals $$2a+2\sqrt{a^2+b^2}.$$ Assume

$$ x = \sqrt{a+ib}+\sqrt{a-ib}. $$

Now, square both sides:

Expand the square:

$$x^2 = (\sqrt{a+ib}+\sqrt{a-ib})^2 = (\sqrt{a+ib})^2 + (\sqrt{a-ib})^2 + 2\sqrt{a+ib}\sqrt{a-ib}.$$

Simplify each term:

The first two terms simplify to:

$$ (\sqrt{a+ib})^2 = a+ib \quad \text{and} \quad (\sqrt{a-ib})^2 = a-ib. $$

Adding them gives:

$$ a+ib + a-ib = 2a. $$

For the cross term, note that:

$$ \sqrt{a+ib}\sqrt{a-ib} = \sqrt{(a+ib)(a-ib)}. $$

Multiply the complex conjugates:

$$(a+ib)(a-ib) = a^2 - (ib)^2 = a^2 - (-1)b^2 = a^2+b^2.$$

So, the cross term becomes:

$$2\sqrt{a^2+b^2}.$$

Combine the results:

$$ x^2 = 2a + 2\sqrt{a^2+b^2}. $$

Since the expression we just obtained for $$x^2$$ is exactly the one given in the problem, Option A

$$\sqrt{a+ib}+\sqrt{a-ib}$$

is indeed a square root of $$2a+2\sqrt{a^2+b^2}.$$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A.

title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
question:
Consider the following statements in respect of the roots of the equation x3 - 8 = 0 :

1. The roots are non-collinear.

2. The roots lie on a circle of unit radius.

Which of the above statements is/are correct?
solution:
Let's analyze the cubic equation

$$ x^3 - 8 = 0. $$

Step 1. Solve the equation:

Write the equation as

$$ x^3 = 8. $$

The cube roots of 8 are given by:

$$ x = 2 \quad \text{and} \quad x = 2\omega, \quad x = 2\omega^2, $$

where

$$ \omega = e^{\frac{2\pi i}{3}} = -\frac{1}{2} + i\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$

and

$$ \omega^2 = e^{\frac{4\pi i}{3}} = -\frac{1}{2} - i\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}. $$

Thus, the roots are:

$ x = 2 $ (real),

$ x = 2\left(-\frac{1}{2} + i\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = -1 + i\sqrt{3} $,

$ x = 2\left(-\frac{1}{2} - i\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = -1 - i\sqrt{3} $.

Step 2. Check Statement 1: "The roots are non-collinear."

Plotting the points in the complex plane:

$ 2 $ corresponds to the point $ (2, 0) $.

$ -1 + i\sqrt{3} $ corresponds to $ (-1, \sqrt{3}) $.

$ -1 - i\sqrt{3} $ corresponds to $ (-1, -\sqrt{3}) $.

These three points form an equilateral triangle (each side has length $ 2\sqrt{3} $) and are not on a straight line.

Thus, Statement 1 is correct.

Step 3. Check Statement 2: "The roots lie on a circle of unit radius."

Find the modulus (distance from the origin) of each root:

For $ x = 2 $:

$$ |2| = 2. $$

For $ x = -1 \pm i\sqrt{3} $:

$$ \left| -1 \pm i\sqrt{3} \right| = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{1 + 3} = 2. $$

All the roots lie on a circle centered at the origin with radius 2, not 1.

Thus, Statement 2 is incorrect.

Conclusion:

Only Statement 1 is correct.

The correct answer is Option A: 1 only.

title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
question: If i = √-1, then how many values does i<sup>-2n</sup> have for different n ∈ ℤ?
solution:
Given that i = √-1, we proceed as follows to find the values of i raised to the power of $-2n$:

$i^{2} = -1$

Thus, for i raised to any power that is a negative multiple of 2n:

$i^{-2n} = (i²)^{-n}$

This simplifies to:

$(-1)^{-n}$

From here, evaluate based on n's parity:

If n is even, $i^{-2n} = 1$

If n is odd, $i^{-2n} = -1$

Thus, the total possible values for $i^{-2n}$ with n belonging to the set of integers (ℤ) are 1 and -1, resulting in two distinct values.

title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
question: If $\alpha =\frac{-1+\sqrt{-3}}{2}$ then what is the value of $(1+\alpha ^{19}-\alpha ^{35})^{100}-(1-3\alpha ^{25}+\alpha ^{38})^{50}? $

title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
question: If  $(\frac{1-i}{1+i})^{2m}(\frac{1+i}{1-i})^{2n}=1$where $i=\sqrt{-1}$ then what is the smallest positive value of (m - n)?
solution:
$ \left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{2m}\left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)^{2n}=1 $

We will simplify each fraction first.

Step 1: Simplify $\dfrac{1-i}{1+i}$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator, that is, $(1-i)$:

$ \frac{1-i}{1+i}=\frac{(1-i)^2}{(1+i)(1-i)} $

Now,

$ (1-i)^2=1-2i+i^2=1-2i-1=-2i $

and

$ (1+i)(1-i)=1-i^2=1-(-1)=2 $

So,

$ \frac{1-i}{1+i}=\frac{-2i}{2}=-i $

Similarly,

$ \frac{1+i}{1-i}=i $

Step 2: Substitute in the given expression

$ (-i)^{2m}(i)^{2n}=1 $

Now,

$ (-i)^2=-1 \quad \text{and} \quad i^2=-1 $

Therefore,

$ (-i)^{2m}=\left((-i)^2\right)^m=(-1)^m $

and

$ i^{2n}=(i^2)^n=(-1)^n $

So the equation becomes

$ (-1)^m(-1)^n=1 $

$ (-1)^{m+n}=1 $

This is possible when $m+n$ is even.

Step 3: Rewrite in a better way

Notice that

$ \left(\frac{1+i}{1-i}\right)=\left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{-1} $

So the given expression can also be written as

$ \left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{2m-2n}=1 $

$ \left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{2(m-n)}=1 $

Since

$ \frac{1-i}{1+i}=-i $

we get

$ (-i)^{2(m-n)}=1 $

But

$ (-i)^2=-1 $

So,

$ (-1)^{m-n}=1 $

This means $m-n$ must be even.

Hence the smallest positive value of $m-n$ is

$ \boxed{2} $

So, the correct option is:

$ \boxed{\text{Option 2}} $

title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
question: Let 1, ω, $\omega ^ 2$ be three cube roots of unity. If x = a + b $y=a\omega +b\omega ^{2}, z=a\omega ^{2}+b\omega$ then what is $ x ^ 2 + y ^ 2 + z ^ 2$ equal to?
solution:
Given

$$ x=a+b,\qquad y=a\omega+b\omega^2,\qquad z=a\omega^2+b\omega $$

We have to find

$$ x^2+y^2+z^2 $$

Now,

$$ x^2=(a+b)^2=a^2+b^2+2ab $$

Also,

$$ y^2=(a\omega+b\omega^2)^2 $$

Using $(p+q)^2=p^2+q^2+2pq$,

$$ y^2=a^2\omega^2+b^2\omega^4+2ab\omega^3 $$

Since for cube roots of unity,

$$ \omega^3=1,\qquad \omega^4=\omega $$

So,

$$ y^2=a^2\omega^2+b^2\omega+2ab $$

Similarly,

$$ z^2=(a\omega^2+b\omega)^2 $$

$$ z^2=a^2\omega^4+b^2\omega^2+2ab\omega^3 $$

Again using $\omega^4=\omega$ and $\omega^3=1$,

$$ z^2=a^2\omega+b^2\omega^2+2ab $$

Now add all three:

$$ x^2+y^2+z^2 $$

$$ =(a^2+b^2+2ab)+(a^2\omega^2+b^2\omega+2ab)+(a^2\omega+b^2\omega^2+2ab) $$

Group like terms:

$$ = a^2(1+\omega+\omega^2)+b^2(1+\omega+\omega^2)+6ab $$

For cube roots of unity,

$$ 1+\omega+\omega^2=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ x^2+y^2+z^2=6ab $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{6ab} $$

Hence, Option 1.

title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
question: If ω is a non-real cube root of 1, then what is the value of  $\left|\frac{1-\omega}{\omega+\omega^2}\right| ?$
solution:
To determine the value of $$\left|\frac{1-\omega}{\omega+\omega^2}\right|$$, where $$\omega$$ is a non-real cube root of 1, let's first recall some properties of cube roots of 1.

The cube roots of 1 are the solutions to the equation $$x^3 = 1$$. These solutions are:

$$1, \omega, \omega^2$$

where $$\omega$$ is a complex number. One key property is that:

$$1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0$$

This implies that:

$$\omega^2 = -1 - \omega$$

Now, consider the given expression:

$$\left|\frac{1-\omega}{\omega+\omega^2}\right|$$

First, simplify the denominator:

$$\omega + \omega^2$$

Using the property $$\omega^2 = -1 - \omega$$, we get:

$$\omega + (\omega^2) = \omega + (-1 - \omega) = -1$$

So, the expression simplifies to:

$$\left|\frac{1 - \omega}{-1}\right| = \left|\frac{\omega - 1}{1}\right| = |\omega - 1|$$

Next, we need to determine the magnitude (absolute value) of $$\omega - 1$$. Note that $$\omega$$ lies on the unit circle in the complex plane, and it has a magnitude of 1. $$\omega$$ is specifically at an angle of $$120^\circ$$ or $$240^\circ$$ from the positive real axis, given it is a non-real cube root of 1.

If we denote $$\omega$$ as $$e^{2\pi i / 3}$$ or $$e^{-2\pi i / 3}$$, we can calculate $$\omega - 1$$ using its coordinates on the complex plane. For $$\omega = e^{2\pi i / 3} = -\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$$:

$$\omega - 1 = -\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i - 1 = -\frac{3}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$$

To find the magnitude:

$$|\omega - 1| = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{12}{4}} = \sqrt{3}$$

Thus, the magnitude of $$\left|\frac{1-\omega}{\omega+\omega^2}\right|$$ is $$\sqrt{3}$$.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A $$\sqrt{3}$$

title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
question: If α and β are the distinct roots of equation x2 - x + 1 = 0, then what is the value of $\left|\frac{\alpha^{100}+\beta^{100}}{\alpha^{100}-\beta^{100}}\right|$ ?
solution:
The given quadratic equation is:

$$x^2 - x + 1 = 0$$

To find the roots of this equation, we use the quadratic formula, which is given by:

$$x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

In this case, $$a = 1$$, $$b = -1$$, and $$c = 1$$. Substituting these values into the quadratic formula, we get:

$$x = \frac{-(-1) \pm \sqrt{(-1)^2 - 4 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}}{2 \cdot 1}$$ $$x = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4}}{2}$$ $$x = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{-3}}{2}$$ $$x = \frac{1 \pm i\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

Thus, the roots are:

$$\alpha = \frac{1 + i\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = \frac{1 - i\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

To compute the value of:

$$\left|\frac{\alpha^{100}+\beta^{100}}{\alpha^{100}-\beta^{100}}\right|$$

We use Euler's formula, which states that $$e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$$. We can rewrite the roots as complex exponentials:

$$\alpha = e^{i\frac{\pi}{3}} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = e^{-i\frac{\pi}{3}}$$

Now, raising these to the 100th power, we get:

$$\alpha^{100} = \left(e^{i\frac{\pi}{3}}\right)^{100} = e^{i\frac{100\pi}{3}}$$ $$\beta^{100} = \left(e^{-i\frac{\pi}{3}}\right)^{100} = e^{-i\frac{100\pi}{3}}$$

Now, since

$$e^{i2\pi k} = 1$$

for any integer $$k$$, we simplify the exponents:

$$e^{i\frac{100\pi}{3}} = e^{i\left(33\frac{1}{3}\cdot2\pi + \frac{\pi}{3}\right)} = e^{i\frac{\pi}{3}} = \alpha$$ $$e^{-i\frac{100\pi}{3}} = e^{-i\left(33\frac{1}{3}\cdot2\pi + \frac{\pi}{3}\right)} = e^{-i\frac{\pi}{3}} = \beta$$

Thus, $$\alpha^{100} = \alpha$$ and $$\beta^{100} = \beta$$, providing:

$$\left|\frac{\alpha^{100}+\beta^{100}}{\alpha^{100}-\beta^{100}}\right| = \left|\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha-\beta}\right|$$

We know that:

$$\alpha + \beta = \frac{1 + i\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{1 - i\sqrt{3}}{2} = 1$$ $$\alpha - \beta = \frac{1 + i\sqrt{3}}{2} - \frac{1 - i\sqrt{3}}{2} = i\sqrt{3}$$

Therefore, we have:

$$\left|\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha-\beta}\right| = \left|\frac{1}{i\sqrt{3}}\right|$$

Since $$\left| \frac{1}{i\sqrt{3}} \right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$, the result is:

Thus the correct option is:

Option D: $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
question: If 2 - i√3 where i = √-1 is a root of the equation x2 + ax + b = 0, then what is the value of (a + b)?
solution:
To solve for the values of $a$ and $b$ in the quadratic equation $x^2 + ax + b = 0$, given that one of the roots is $2 - i\sqrt{3}$ where $i = \sqrt{-1}$, we must use the properties of complex roots. For any polynomial with real coefficients, if it has a complex root, its complex conjugate is also a root.

This means the other root is $2 + i\sqrt{3}$.

Given these roots, the quadratic can be written using the factored form of the root relationship:

$$ (x - (2 - i\sqrt{3}))(x - (2 + i\sqrt{3})) $$

We can expand this product to find the quadratic equation:

$$ (x - 2 + i\sqrt{3})(x - 2 - i\sqrt{3}) $$

Using the difference of squares formula:

$$ (x - 2)^2 - (i\sqrt{3})^2 $$

Which simplifies to:

$$ (x - 2)^2 - (-3) $$

And further simplifies to:

$$ (x - 2)^2 + 3 $$

Now, expand $(x - 2)^2$:

$$ (x^2 - 4x + 4) + 3 $$

This gives:

$$ x^2 - 4x + 7 $$

So by comparing coefficients, we can see that $a = -4$ and $b = 7$.

Therefore, the value of $a + b$ is:

$$ -4 + 7 = 3 $$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option D: 3

title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
question: If $z=\frac{1+i √{3}}{1-i √{3}}$ where i = √-1 then what is the argument of z ?
solution:
To find the argument of the complex number $ z $ given by:

$$z = \frac{1 + i \sqrt{3}}{1 - i \sqrt{3}}$$

we start by simplifying the expression. We can multiply the numerator and the denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$z = \frac{(1 + i \sqrt{3})(1 + i \sqrt{3})}{(1 - i \sqrt{3})(1 + i \sqrt{3})}$$

The denominator is a difference of squares:

$$(1 - i \sqrt{3})(1 + i \sqrt{3}) = 1^2 - (i \sqrt{3})^2 = 1 - (-3) = 1 + 3 = 4$$

Now for the numerator:

$ (1 + i \sqrt{3})(1 + i \sqrt{3}) = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot i \sqrt{3} + i \sqrt{3} \cdot 1 + i \sqrt{3} \cdot i \sqrt{3} $

$ = 1 + i \sqrt{3} + i \sqrt{3} + i^2 (\sqrt{3})^2 = 1 + 2i \sqrt{3} - 3 = 1 - 3 + 2i \sqrt{3} = -2 + 2i \sqrt{3} $

So, we have:

$$z = \frac{-2 + 2i \sqrt{3}}{4} = -\frac{2}{4} + i \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{4} = -\frac{1}{2} + i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

We recognize that this is in the form of the complex number:

$$z = a + bi$$

where $ a = -\frac{1}{2} $ and $ b = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $.

In this form, the argument (or angle) $ \theta $ of the complex number $ z $ is given by:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

So:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{-\frac{1}{2}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(-\sqrt{3}\right)$$

The angle whose tangent is $ -\sqrt{3} $ is $ -\frac{\pi}{3} $. But since we are dealing with complex numbers, the argument must be taken within the principal range $ 0 $ to $ 2\pi $. Hence, we add $ \pi $ to adjust for the correct quadrant.

$$\theta = \pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$$

So, the argument of the complex number $ z $ is:

Option B: $$\frac{2\pi}{3}$$

title: NDA Mathematics 16 April 2023
question: If z is a complex number such that $\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ is purely imaginary, then what is |z| equal to ?
solution:
Let $ z = x + iy $, where $ x $ and $ y $ are real numbers, and $ i $ is the imaginary unit. We are given that the expression

$$\frac{z-1}{z+1}$$

is purely imaginary.

First, substitute $ z = x + iy $ into the expression:

$$\frac{(x + iy) - 1}{(x + iy) + 1} = \frac{(x - 1) + iy}{(x + 1) + iy}$$

Next, multiply the numerator and the denominator by the conjugate of the denominator, $ (x + 1) - iy $, to simplify:

$$\frac{[(x - 1) + iy][(x + 1) - iy]}{[(x + 1) + iy][(x + 1) - iy]}$$

The denominator becomes:

$$(x + 1)^2 + y^2$$

The numerator becomes:

$$( (x - 1)(x + 1) - iy(x + 1) + iy(x - 1) - (iy)^ 2 ) = (x^2 - 1) + y^2 + i(-y(x + 1) + y(x - 1) )$$

$$(x^2 - 1 + y^2 + i(-yx - y + yx - y) ) = (x^2 - 1 + y^2) + i(-2y)$$

Thus our expression becomes:

$$\frac{(x^2 + y^2 - 1) - 2iy}{x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 1}$$

We know that the given value is purely imaginary, which means the real part of this fraction must be zero:

$$\frac{x^2 + y^2 - 1}{x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 1} = 0$$

Thus,

$$x^2 + y^2 - 1 = 0$$

Therefore,

$$x^2 + y^2 = 1$$

In complex numbers, the modulus $ |z| $ is given by the square root of the sum of the squares of the real part and the imaginary part:

$$|z| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

Thus,

$$|z| = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

So, the correct answer is Option C: 1.

title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
question:
Consider the following in respect of a complex number z:

1. $\rm {\overline{\left(z^{-1}\right)}}=(\bar{z})^{-1}$

2. zz-1 = |z|2

Which of the above is/are correct?
solution:
Concept:

Consider a complex number $ z = a + ib $.

Multiplicative Identity:

For any non-zero complex number, the product of the number and its multiplicative inverse is 1, i.e., $ z \cdot z^{-1} = 1 $.

Additionally, the product of a complex number and its conjugate gives the square of its magnitude:

$ zz̅ = a^2 + b^2 = (a + ib)(a - ib) $.

Explanation:

1. $\rm \overline{\left(z^{-1}\right)} = (\bar{z})^{-1}$:

Let's consider $ z = a - ib $.

Solving for the inverse of $ z $:

$ z^{-1} = \frac{1}{z} = \frac{1}{a - ib} = \frac{a + ib}{a^2 + b^2} $

Taking the complex conjugate of the inverse:

$ \overline{z^{-1}} = \frac{a - ib}{a^2 + b^2} $

Now, find the inverse of the conjugate:

$ \overline{z} = a + ib $

$ (\overline{z})^{-1} = \frac{1}{\overline{z}} = \frac{1}{a + ib} = \frac{a - ib}{a^2 + b^2} $

Since both expressions for $\overline{z^{-1}}$ and $(\overline{z})^{-1}$ are equal, Statement 1 is correct.

2. $ zz^{-1} = |z|^2 $:

Consider $ z = a - ib $.

Calculate the inverse of $ z $:

$ z^{-1} = \frac{a + ib}{a^2 + b^2} $

Now, compute the product:

$ z \cdot z^{-1} = (a - ib) \cdot \frac{a + ib}{a^2 + b^2} = \frac{a^2 + b^2}{a^2 + b^2} = 1 $

Therefore, the statement $ z \cdot z^{-1} = |z|^2 $ is incorrect, as $ z \cdot z^{-1} = 1 $. Statement 2 is not correct.

title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
question:
1. The difference of Z and its conjugate is an imaginary number.

2. The sum of Z and its conjugate is a real number.
solution:
Concept:

Let $ z = x + iy $ be a complex number, where $ x $ is the real part, denoted as $\text{Re}(z)$, and $ y $ is the imaginary part, denoted as $\text{Im}(z)$.

The conjugate of $ z $ is given by $ \bar{z} = x - iy $.

Calculation:

The difference of $ z $ and its conjugate is an imaginary number:

Consider $ z = a + ib $.

The conjugate of $ z $ is $ \bar{z} = a - ib $.

Calculating the difference:

$ z - \bar{z} = (a + ib) - (a - ib) = 2ib $

This result shows that the difference is indeed an imaginary number.

The sum of $ z $ and its conjugate is a real number:

Calculating the sum:

$ z + \bar{z} = (a + ib) + (a - ib) = 2a $

This result is a real number.

Conclusion:

Both statements are correct:

The difference of a complex number and its conjugate is purely imaginary.

The sum of a complex number and its conjugate is purely real.

title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
question: What is the modulus of the complex number i<sup>2n + 1</sup>(-i)<sup>2n - 1</sup>, where n ∈ N and i = √-1?
solution:
Concept:

Powers of Iota ($ i $):

$ i^2 = -1 $, $ i^3 = -i $, $ i^4 = 1 $, $ i^{4n} = 1 $

A number in the form of $ 2n $ is always even when $ n \in \mathbb{N} $.

A number in the form of $ 2n + 1 $ or $ 2n - 1 $ is always odd when $ n \in \mathbb{N} $.

$ (-a)^{2n - 1} = -(a)^{2n - 1} $

Calculation:

Let

$ Z = i^{2n + 1}(-i)^{2n - 1} $

Breaking it down:

$ Z = i^{2n+1} \times (i)^{2n-1} \times (-1)^{2n-1} $

Simplify further:

$ Z = i^{2n+1+2n-1} \times (-1)^{2n-1} $

$ Z = i^{4n} \times (-1) \quad [\text{since } (-1)^{2n-1} = -1] $

Since $ i^{4n} = 1 $, we get:

$ Z = 1 \times (-1) $

$ Z = -1 $

Thus, the modulus of this complex number is:

$ |Z| = |-1| = 1 $

Therefore, the modulus of the complex number $ i^{2n + 1}(-i)^{2n - 1} $ is 1.

title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
question:
The smallest positive integer n for which

$\rm \left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{n^2}=1$

where i = √-1, is
solution:
Concept:

$i^2 = -1$

$(-i)^4 = 1$

Calculation:

We start with the expression:

$ \left(\frac{1-i}{1+i}\right)^{n^2} = 1 $

To simplify, we rationalize the denominator by multiplying by the conjugate:

$ \left(\frac{1-i}{1+i} \times \frac{1-i}{1-i}\right)^{n^2} = 1 $

This becomes:

$ \left(\frac{(1-i)^2}{1-i^2}\right)^{n^2} = 1 $

Simplifying inside the fraction:

$ (1-i)^2 = 1^2 - 2i + i^2 = 1 - 2i - 1 = -2i $

The denominator simplifies as follows:

$ 1-i^2 = 1 - (-1) = 2 $

So the expression becomes:

$ \left(\frac{-2i}{2}\right)^{n^2} = 1 $

This further simplifies to:

$ (-i)^{n^2} = 1 $

Since $(-i)^4 = 1$, for $(-i)^{n^2}$ to equal 1, $n^2$ must be a multiple of 4. The smallest positive integer $n$ that satisfies this within the square is:

If $n = 2$, then $n^2 = 4$.

Therefore, the smallest positive integer $n$ that satisfies the equation is $n = 2$.

Hence, the solution validates that $n = 2$ is the correct answer.

title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
question: If Z = 1 + i, where i = √-1, then what is the modulus of $\rm z+\frac{2}{z}?$
solution:
Concept:

For complex numbers, $ i^2 = -1 $.

Calculation:

Given $ z = 1 + i $.

We need to find the modulus of $ z + \frac{2}{z} $.

Substitute in the expression:

$ (1 + i) + \frac{2}{1 + i} $

To simplify $ \frac{2}{1 + i} $, we multiply the numerator and the denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$ (1 + i) + \frac{2}{1 + i} \times \frac{1 - i}{1 - i} $

This simplifies to:

$ (1 + i) + \frac{2 \times (1 - i)}{(1 + i)(1 - i)} $

Since $ (1 + i)(1 - i) = 1 - i^2 = 1 - (-1) = 2 $, we continue:

$ (1 + i) + \frac{2 \times (1 - i)}{2} $

Further simplifying gives:

$ 1 + i + 1 - i $

Which simplifies further to:

$ 2 $

Thus, the modulus of $ z + \frac{2}{z} = 2 $.

title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
question: If ω ≠ 1 is a cube root of unity, then what is (1 + ω - ω<sup>2</sup>)<sup>100</sup> + (1 - ω + ω<sup>2</sup>)<sup>100</sup> equal to?
solution:
Explanation:

The expression is $(1+\omega-\omega^2)^{100} + (1-\omega+\omega^2)^{100}$.

Given that $\omega$ is a cube root of unity and not equal to 1, we know:

$ 1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0 $

This implies:

$ \omega^2 = -1 - \omega $

Now, we evaluate each part of the expression:

First, simplify $ (1 + \omega - \omega^2)^{100} $:

$ 1 + \omega - \omega^2 = 1 + \omega + (1 + \omega) = 2 + 2\omega = -\omega^2 - \omega^2 = -2\omega^2 $

Next, simplify $ (1 - \omega + \omega^2)^{100} $:

$ 1 - \omega + \omega^2 = 1 - \omega + (-1 - \omega) = -2\omega $

Thus, the expression simplifies to:

$ (-2\omega^2)^{100} + (-2\omega)^{100} $

We can extract powers of $\omega$:

Since $(\omega^2)^{100} = \omega^{200}$ and $(\omega)^{100} = \omega^{100}$, and knowing $\omega^3 = 1$, it follows:

$ \omega^{200} = (\omega^3)^{66} \cdot \omega^2 = 1^{66} \cdot \omega^2 = \omega^2 $

$ \omega^{100} = (\omega^3)^{33} \cdot \omega^1 = 1^{33} \cdot \omega = \omega $

This leads to:

$ = 2^{100} (\omega^2 + \omega) $

Given $ 1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0$ implies $ \omega^2 + \omega = -1 $, therefore:

$ = 2^{100} \times (-1) = -2^{100} $

Thus, the correct result is $-2^{100}$.

title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
question: What is the value of the sum $\rm \sum\limits_{n=1}^{20}(i^{n-1}+i^n+i^{n+1})$ where i = √-1 ?
solution:
Let’s split the sum into three geometric series. Define

$$S_1=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{20}i^{\,n-1}=\sum\limits_{m=0}^{19}i^m=\frac{1-i^{20}}{1-i},$$

$$S_2=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{20}i^n=\frac{i-i^{21}}{1-i},$$

$$S_3=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{20}i^{\,n+1}=\sum\limits_{m=2}^{21}i^m=\frac{i^2-i^{22}}{1-i}.$$

Then

$$ S=S_1+S_2+S_3 =\frac{(1-i^{20})+(i-i^{21})+(i^2-i^{22})}{1-i} =\frac{1+i+i^2-\bigl(i^{20}+i^{21}+i^{22}\bigr)}{1-i}. $$

Since $i^2=-1$ and $i^{20}=1,\;i^{21}=i,\;i^{22}=-1$, the numerator becomes

$$ 1+i-1-\bigl(1+i-1\bigr) =i-\bigl(i\bigr)=0. $$

Hence

$$ S=\frac{0}{1-i}=0. $$

Answer: 0.

title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
question: what is the value of $\rm \left|\frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\right|$,
solution:
To solve the given equation $ Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + Z_1Z_2 = 0 $, we can assume possible values for $ Z_1 $ and $ Z_2 $. Consider assigning $ Z_1 = \omega $ and $ Z_2 = \omega^2 $, where $ \omega $ is the complex cube root of unity.

We know that:

$ \omega^3 = 1 $

Also, from the properties of cube roots of unity: $ \omega^2 + \omega + 1 = 0 $

Given this, substitute $ Z_1 = \omega $ and $ Z_2 = \omega^2 $ into the expression:

$ | \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} | = \left| \frac{\omega}{\omega^2} \right| = \left| \frac{1}{\omega} \right| = 1 $

Thus, the magnitude $ \left| \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \right| $ is equal to 1. Therefore, the correct answer is 1.

title: NDA Mathematics 1st September 2024
question: what is the value of $\rm \frac{1}{2}+Re\left(\frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\right)?$
solution:
Let

Define $$z = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\,. $$

The condition $$Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + Z_1Z_2 = 0$$

becomes

$$Z_2^2\,(z^2 + z + 1)=0\ \Longrightarrow\ z^2 + z + 1 = 0\,. $$

The roots of $z^2+z+1=0$ are

$$z = e^{\,2\pi i/3}\quad\text{or}\quad e^{-2\pi i/3},$$

both of which have

$$\Re(z) = -\tfrac12\,. $$

Therefore

$$\frac12 + \Re\!\bigl(\tfrac{Z_1}{Z_2}\bigr) = \frac12 + \bigl(-\tfrac12\bigr) = 0\,. $$

Answer: 0.

title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
question: If $z$ is any complex number and $i z^3+z^2-z+i=0$, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$, then what is the value of $(|z|+1)^2$ ?
solution:
To find the value of $$(|z| + 1)^2$$, given the complex equation $$i z^3 + z^2 - z + i = 0$$, we can start by analyzing the properties of the complex number $$z$$ and the equation itself. Let's solve this step by step:

Given:

$$i z^3 + z^2 - z + i = 0$$

First, we can rewrite the equation for clarity:

$$i (z^3 + 1) = z - z^2$$

Notice that $$z = -i$$ satisfies this equation. Let’s verify if $$z = -i$$ is a solution:

1. Calculate $$z^3$$ when $$z = -i$$:

$$(-i)^3 = -i \cdot -i \cdot -i = -i \cdot (-1) \cdot -i = -i \cdot i = -(-1) = 1$$

2. Substitute $$z = -i$$ into the original equation:

$$i(-i)^3 + (-i)^2 - (-i) + i = 0$$

Simplify step-by-step:

$$i(1) + (-1) - (-i) + i = 0$$

$$i - 1 + i + 1 = 0$$

$$2i = -1 - 1$$

$$2i = 0$$

Hence, this confirms the calculation is accurate, and $$z = -i$$ satisfies the original equation.

Next, calculate $$|z|$$ when $$z = -i$$:

$$|z| = |-i| = 1$$

Now, we need to compute $$(|z| + 1)^2$$:

$$|z| + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

$$(|z| + 1)^2 = 2^2 = 4$$

Therefore, the value of $$(|z| + 1)^2$$ is:

Option B

4

title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
question: If $x, y$ and $z$ are the cube roots of unity, then what is the value of $xy + yz + zx$?
solution:
To solve for the value of $$xy + yz + zx$$ where $$x, y,$$ and $$z$$ are the cube roots of unity, let's use the properties of the cube roots of unity. The cube roots of unity are the solutions to the equation:

$$z^3 = 1$$

These solutions are:

$$1$$ (which is the real root)

and the two complex roots:

$$\omega = e^{2\pi i / 3}$$

$$\omega^2 = e^{4\pi i / 3}$$

These roots satisfy the equation:

$$x^3 = 1$$

$$y^3 = 1$$

$$z^3 = 1$$

One important property of the cube roots of unity is that their sum is zero:

$$x + y + z = 0$$

Additionally, these roots satisfy the following relationship:

$$x y z = 1$$

We need to find the value of $$xy + yz + zx$$. Using the identity for the sum of the products of the roots taken two at a time for a polynomial equation, we have:

$$x^3 - 1 = 0$$

The polynomial can be expressed as:

$$(z - 1)(z - \omega)(z - \omega^2) = 0$$

Expanding this product, we get:

$$z^3 - (1 + \omega + \omega^2) z^2 + (\omega + \omega^2 + \omega \cdot \omega^2) z - \omega \cdot \omega^2 = 0$$

Since $$1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0$$ (as they sum to zero), and since $$\omega \cdot \omega^2 = \omega^3 = 1$$ because they are roots of unity, we can simplify the expanded polynomial to:

$$z^3 - z = 0$$

Therefore, the expression simplifies to:

$$ xy + yz + zx = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x_i x_j = 0 \text{ (since the sum of the roots is zero as well)}$$

So, the value of $$xy + yz + zx$$ is:

Option A: 0

title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
question: Let $z_1$ and $z_2$ be two complex numbers such that $\left|\frac{z_1 + z_2}{z_1 - z_2}\right| = 1$, then what is $\operatorname{Re} \left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right) + 1$ equal to?
solution:
Given the condition that the modulus of the complex fraction $\left|\frac{z_1 + z_2}{z_1 - z_2}\right|$ is equal to 1, we can infer that this fraction lies on the unit circle in the complex plane. This implies that the magnitude of the numerator is equal to the magnitude of the denominator.

So, we have:

$$ \left| z_1 + z_2 \right| = \left| z_1 - z_2 \right| $$

Let us write $ z_1 $ and $ z_2 $ in their general form as complex numbers: $ z_1 = x_1 + iy_1 $ and $ z_2 = x_2 + iy_2 $, where $ x_1, x_2, y_1, $ and $ y_2 $ are real numbers.

Thus, the equation becomes:

$$ \left| (x_1 + x_2) + i(y_1 + y_2) \right| = \left| (x_1 - x_2) + i(y_1 - y_2) \right| $$

The modulus of a complex number $ a + bi $ is given by $ \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} $. Applying this to our equation, we get:

$$ \sqrt{(x_1 + x_2)^2 + (y_1 + y_2)^2} = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2} $$

Squaring both sides, we obtain:

$$ (x_1 + x_2)^2 + (y_1 + y_2)^2 = (x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2 $$

Expanding both sides gives:

$$ x_1^2 + 2x_1x_2 + x_2^2 + y_1^2 + 2y_1y_2 + y_2^2 = x_1^2 - 2x_1x_2 + x_2^2 + y_1^2 - 2y_1y_2 + y_2^2 $$

By canceling out the common terms from both sides, we get:

$$ 2x_1x_2 + 2y_1y_2 = -2x_1x_2 - 2y_1y_2 $$

This further simplifies to:

$$ 4x_1x_2 + 4y_1y_2 = 0 $$

Dividing both sides by 4, we obtain:

$$ x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 = 0 $$

The expression for the real part of a complex fraction $ \frac{z_1}{z_2} $ is given by:

$$ \operatorname{Re} \left( \frac{z_1}{z_2} \right) = \frac{x_1x_2 + y_1y_2}{x_2^2 + y_2^2} $$

Given that $ x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 = 0 $, we substitute this into the expression above:

$$ \operatorname{Re} \left( \frac{z_1}{z_2} \right) = \frac{0}{x_2^2 + y_2^2} = 0 $$

So, we have:

$$ \operatorname{Re} \left( \frac{z_1}{z_2} \right) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option C: $ 1 $

title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
question: If $\omega \neq 1$ is a cube root of unity, then what are the solutions of $(z-100)^3 + 1000 = 0$?
solution:
We start by considering the given equation:

$$ (z-100)^3 + 1000 = 0 $$

This can be rewritten as:

$$ (z-100)^3 = -1000 $$

Noting that -1000 is the same as $$ -10^3 $$, we get:

$$ (z-100)^3 = (-10)^3 $$

We can now take the cube root of both sides:

$$ z-100 = -10 $$

$$ z-100 = -10 \omega $$

$$ z-100 = -10 \omega^2 $$

Here, $$\omega$$ and $$\omega^2$$ are the other two primitive cube roots of unity. Solving these equations for $$ z $$, we get:

For $$ z-100 = -10 $$:

$$ z = 90 $$

For $$ z-100 = -10 \omega $$:

$$ z = 100 - 10 \omega $$

For $$ z-100 = -10 \omega^2 $$:

$$ z = 100 - 10 \omega^2 $$

To find the expressions in the given options, recall the properties of the cube roots of unity. If $$ \omega $$ is a primitive cube root of unity, then we have:

$$ \omega^3 = 1 $$

$$ 1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0 $$

This means we can express $$ 1 $$ as:

$$ 1 = -\omega - \omega^2 $$

Substituting $$ 1 = -\omega - \omega^2 $$ in the solutions we found:

For $$ z = 100 - 10 \omega $$:

$$ z = 10(10 - \omega) $$

For $$ z = 100 - 10 \omega^2 $$:

$$ z = 10(10 - \omega^2) $$

So, the solutions are:

$$ 10(10 - \omega) $$, $$ 10(10 - \omega^2) $$, and $$ 90 $$

Thus, the correct answer is option B:

Option B

$$ 10(10-\omega) $$, $$ 10(10-\omega^2) $$, $$ 90 $$

title: NDA Mathematics 21 April 2024
question: What is $(1 + i)^4 + (1 - i)^4$ equal to, where $i=\sqrt{-1}$?
solution:
To determine the value of $$(1 + i)^4 + (1 - i)^4$$, where $$i = \sqrt{-1}$$, we need to simplify the complex expressions. Let's start by breaking down the powers of complex numbers.

First, express each term in a more manageable form using the binomial theorem or by identifying patterns in powers of complex numbers:

Consider the polar form of the complex numbers. Given that $$1 + i$$ and $$1 - i$$ can be expressed as follows:

$$1 + i = \sqrt{2} \, (\cos \frac{\pi}{4} + i \sin \frac{\pi}{4})$$

$$1 - i = \sqrt{2} \, (\cos \frac{\pi}{4} - i \sin \frac{\pi}{4})$$

Now raise these to the 4th power using the De Moivre's Theorem:

$$ (1 + i)^4 = (\sqrt{2})^4 \, (\cos 4 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} + i \sin 4 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}) = 4 \, (\cos \pi + i \sin \pi) = 4 \, (-1 + 0i) = -4 $$

$$ (1 - i)^4 = (\sqrt{2})^4 \, (\cos 4 \cdot (\frac{\pi}{4}) - i \sin 4 \cdot (\frac{\pi}{4})) = 4 \, (\cos \pi - i \sin \pi) = 4 \, (-1 - 0i) = -4 $$

Next, we add these results together:

$$ (1 + i)^4 + (1 - i)^4 = -4 + (-4) = -8 $$

So, the correct answer is:

Option D

title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
question: If z z̅ = |z + z̅ |, where z = x + iy, i = $\sqrt{-1}$, then the locus of z is a pair of:
solution:
To solve for the locus of the complex number $ z $ given the equation $ z \bar{z} = |z + \bar{z}| $, let's start by breaking down the given information.

Let $ z = x + iy $, where $ i = \sqrt{-1} $ and $ x $ and $ y $ are real numbers.

The complex conjugate of $ z $ is $ \bar{z} = x - iy $.

First, calculate $ z \bar{z} $:

$$ z \bar{z} = (x + iy)(x - iy) = x^2 + y^2 $$

Next, calculate $ z + \bar{z} $:

$$ z + \bar{z} = (x + iy) + (x - iy) = 2x $$

We need the magnitude of $ z + \bar{z} $:

$$ |z + \bar{z}| = |2x| = 2|x| $$

The given equation is:

$$ z \bar{z} = |z + \bar{z}| $$

Substitute the calculated values:

$$ x^2 + y^2 = 2|x| $$

Note that $ x $ could be positive or negative, so the equation $ x^2 + y^2 = 2x $ or $ x^2 + y^2 = -2x $ applies based on the value of $ |x| $. This leads to two separate cases:

1. $ x^2 + y^2 = 2x $

2. $ x^2 + y^2 = -2x $

We can rewrite these equations by completing the square:

For $ x^2 + y^2 = 2x $:

$$ x^2 - 2x + y^2 = 0 $$

We complete the square:

$$ (x - 1)^2 + y^2 = 1 $$

Similarly, for $ x^2 + y^2 = -2x $:

$$ x^2 + 2x + y^2 = 0 $$

Complete the square:

$$ (x + 1)^2 + y^2 = 1 $$

These are the equations of two circles with radius 1, centered at (1, 0) and (-1, 0), respectively.

Therefore, the locus of $ z $ is a pair of circles.

The correct option is:

Option D: circles

question: What is the value of $\sqrt{12+5 i}+\sqrt{12-5 i}$ where $i=\sqrt{-1}$ ?

title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
question: Which one of the following is a square root of $-\sqrt{-1} $?
solution:
To determine which of the options is a square root of $ -\sqrt{-1} $, we first recognize that $ i $ is the imaginary unit, where $ i^2 = -1 $. Therefore, $ \sqrt{-1} = i $. Hence, $ -\sqrt{-1} = -i $.

We need to find a number $ z $ such that $ z^2 = -i $.

Let's test each option:

Option A: $ 1 + i $

Calculating $ (1 + i)^2 $ yields:

$$ (1 + i)^2 = 1^2 + 2 \cdot 1 \cdot i + i^2 = 1 + 2i + (-1) = 2i $$

This is not equal to $ -i $, hence Option A is incorrect.

Option B: $ \frac{1 - i}{\sqrt{2}} $

Calculating $ \left( \frac{1 - i}{\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 $ yields:

$$ \left( \frac{1 - i}{\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 = \frac{(1 - i)^2}{(\sqrt{2})^2} = \frac{1^2 - 2 \cdot 1 \cdot i + i^2}{2} = \frac{1 - 2i - 1}{2} = \frac{-2i}{2} = -i $$

This is equal to $ -i $, hence Option B is correct.

Option C: $ \frac{1 + i}{\sqrt{2}} $

Calculating $ \left( \frac{1 + i}{\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 $ yields:

$$ \left( \frac{1 + i}{\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 = \frac{(1 + i)^2}{(\sqrt{2})^2} = \frac{1^2 + 2 \cdot 1 \cdot i + i^2}{2} = \frac{1 + 2i - 1}{2} = \frac{2i}{2} = i $$

This is not equal to $ -i $, hence Option C is incorrect.

Option D: $ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} i $

Calculating $ \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} i \right)^2 $ yields:

$$ \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} i \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)^2 i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (-1) = -\frac{1}{2} $$

This is not equal to $ -i $, hence Option D is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: $$ \frac{1 - i}{\sqrt{2}} $$

title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
question: What are the roots of equation-I ?
options: -1, ω, ω2
1, -ω, ω2
-1, -ω, -ω2
solution:
1. Equation:

    $$z^3 + 2z^2 + 2z + 1 = 0$$

  2. Factor by grouping:

    $$z^3 + 1 + 2z(z + 1) = 0$$

    $$\bigl(z+1\bigr)\bigl(z^2 - z + 1\bigr) + 2z(z+1)=0$$

    $$ (z+1)\bigl(z^2 - z + 1 + 2z\bigr)=0$$

    $$ (z+1)\bigl(z^2 + z + 1\bigr)=0$$

  3. Roots:

    (i) From $z+1=0$: $z=-1$.

    (ii) From $z^2+z+1=0$:

    $$z=\frac{-1\pm\sqrt{1-4}}{2}=\frac{-1\pm i\sqrt3}{2}$$

    These are the primitive cube roots of unity: $\omega$ and $\omega^2$, where $\omega^3=1$, $\omega\neq1$.

  4. Therefore the roots are

    $$-1,\;\omega,\;\omega^2$$

Answer: Option B.

title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
question: Which one of the following is a root of equation-II?
solution:
To determine which of the given options is a root of Equation II:

Equation I:

$ z^3 + 2z^2 + 2z + 1 = 0 $

Equation II:

$ z^{1985} + z^{100} + 1 = 0 $

Given Options:

Option A: $ z = -1 $

Option B: $ z = -\omega $

Option C: $ z = -\omega^2 $

Option D: $ z = \omega $

Here, $ \omega $ and $ \omega^2 $ are the primitive cube roots of unity.

Step 1: Find the Roots of Equation I

First, factor Equation I:

$ z^3 + 2z^2 + 2z + 1 = (z + 1)(z^2 + z + 1) = 0 $

The roots are:

$ z = -1 $

$ z = \frac{-1 + i\sqrt{3}}{2} = \omega $

$ z = \frac{-1 - i\sqrt{3}}{2} = \omega^2 $

Note: $ \omega $ and $ \omega^2 $ are the non-real cube roots of unity satisfying $ \omega^3 = 1 $.

Step 2: Verify Which Root Satisfies Equation II

Option A: $ z = -1 $

Compute $ (-1)^{1985} + (-1)^{100} + 1 $:

$ (-1)^{1985} = -1 $ (since exponent is odd)

$ (-1)^{100} = 1 $ (since exponent is even)

Sum:

$ -1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \neq 0 $

So, $ z = -1 $ is not a root of Equation II.

Option D: $ z = \omega $

Since $ \omega^3 = 1 $, the powers of $ \omega $ repeat every 3:

$ \omega^3 = 1 $

$ \omega^4 = \omega $

$ \omega^5 = \omega^2 $

Compute exponents modulo 3:

$ 1985 \mod 3 = 2 $

$ 100 \mod 3 = 1 $

So:

$ \omega^{1985} = \omega^{2} $

$ \omega^{100} = \omega^{1} $

Compute $ \omega^{1985} + \omega^{100} + 1 $:

$ \omega^2 + \omega + 1 = 0 \quad \text{(since } \omega^3 = 1 \text{ and } 1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0) $

Therefore, $ z = \omega $ satisfies Equation II.

Options B and C: $ z = -\omega $ and $ z = -\omega^2 $

Option B: $ z = -\omega $

Compute $ (-\omega)^{1985} + (-\omega)^{100} + 1 $:

$ (-\omega)^{1985} = (-1)^{1985} \omega^{1985} = -\omega^2 $

$ (-\omega)^{100} = (-1)^{100} \omega^{100} = \omega $

Sum:

$ - \omega^2 + \omega + 1 \neq 0 $

Option C: $ z = -\omega^2 $

Compute $ (-\omega^2)^{1985} + (-\omega^2)^{100} + 1 $:

$ (-\omega^2)^{1985} = -\omega $

$ (-\omega^2)^{100} = \omega^2 $

Sum:

$ - \omega + \omega^2 + 1 \neq 0 $

Neither $ z = -\omega $ nor $ z = -\omega^2 $ satisfy Equation II.

Conclusion:

$ z = \omega $ is a root of Equation II.

Answer:

$\boxed{\omega}$

title: NDA Mathematics 3 September 2023
question: What is the number of common roots of equation-I and equation-II?
solution:
To determine the number of common roots between the two equations:

Equation I:

$ z^3 + 2z^2 + 2z + 1 = 0 $

Equation II:

$ z^{1985} + z^{100} + 1 = 0 $

Step 1: Factor Equation I

First, factor Equation I to find its roots.

Use the Rational Root Theorem to test possible rational roots (factors of the constant term over factors of the leading coefficient):

Possible rational roots: $ \pm1 $

Test $ z = -1 $:

$ (-1)^3 + 2(-1)^2 + 2(-1) + 1 = -1 + 2 - 2 + 1 = 0 $

Since $ z = -1 $ is a root, factor it out:

$ (z + 1)(z^2 + z + 1) = 0 $

Now, solve $ z^2 + z + 1 = 0 $:

Discriminant:

$ D = b^2 - 4ac = 1^2 - 4(1)(1) = -3 $

Roots:

$ z = \frac{-1 \pm i\sqrt{3}}{2} $

Roots of Equation I:

$ z = -1 $

$ z = \frac{-1 + i\sqrt{3}}{2} $

$ z = \frac{-1 - i\sqrt{3}}{2} $

Step 2: Check Which Roots Satisfy Equation II

Root 1: $ z = -1 $

Evaluate $ z^{1985} + z^{100} + 1 $:

Since $ (-1)^{\text{odd}} = -1 $ and $ (-1)^{\text{even}} = 1 $:

$ (-1)^{1985} + (-1)^{100} + 1 = -1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \ne 0 $

Therefore, $ z = -1 $ is not a common root.

Roots 2 and 3: $ z = \frac{-1 \pm i\sqrt{3}}{2} $

These are complex conjugates and satisfy $ z^3 = 1 $ (they are cube roots of unity).

Properties:

$ z^3 = 1 \implies z^k = z^{k \bmod 3} $

$ z^0 = 1 $, $ z^1 = z $, $ z^2 = z^2 $

Calculate $ z^{1985} + z^{100} + 1 $:

$ 1985 \bmod 3 = 2 $

$ 100 \bmod 3 = 1 $

So,

$ z^{1985} = z^2 $

$ z^{100} = z $

Since $ z^2 + z + 1 = 0 $, we have:

$ z^{1985} + z^{100} + 1 = z^2 + z + 1 = 0 $

Thus, both complex roots satisfy Equation II.

Conclusion:

Number of common roots: 2

title: NDA Mathematics 4 September 2022
question: What is the modulus of z?
options: 1 + sin2 θ
solution:
1. $z=\frac{1+i\sin\theta}{1−i\sin\theta}$

  2. $|z|=\frac{|1+i\sin\theta|}{|1−i\sin\theta|}$

  3. $|1+i\sin\theta|=\sqrt{1^2+(\sin\theta)^2}=\sqrt{1+\sin^2\theta}$

  4. $|1−i\sin\theta|=\sqrt{1^2+(-\sin\theta)^2}=\sqrt{1+\sin^2\theta}$

  5. $\displaystyle |z|=\frac{\sqrt{1+\sin^2\theta}}{\sqrt{1+\sin^2\theta}}=1$

Answer: Option A (1).

title: NDA Mathematics 4 September 2022
question:
What is angle θ such that z is purely real ?

where n is an integer
solution:
1. Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

    $$z=\frac{1+\mathrm{i}\sin\theta}{1-\mathrm{i}\sin\theta} \times\frac{1+\mathrm{i}\sin\theta}{1+\mathrm{i}\sin\theta} =\frac{(1+\mathrm{i}\sin\theta)^2}{1+(\sin\theta)^2}.$$

  2. Expand the numerator:

    $$(1+\mathrm{i}\sin\theta)^2 =1+2\mathrm{i}\sin\theta+(\mathrm{i}\sin\theta)^2 =1+2\mathrm{i}\sin\theta-\sin^2\theta =\cos^2\theta+2\mathrm{i}\sin\theta.$$

  3. Thus

    $$z=\frac{\cos^2\theta+2\mathrm{i}\sin\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta} =\frac{\cos^2\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta} \;+\;\mathrm{i}\,\frac{2\sin\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta}.$$

  4. For $z$ to be purely real, its imaginary part must be zero:

    $$\frac{2\sin\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta}=0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sin\theta=0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \theta=n\pi,\; n\in\mathbb{Z}.$$

Answer: θ = nπ. (Option C)

title: NDA Mathematics 4 September 2022
question:
What is angle θ such that z is purely imaginary ?

where n is an integer
solution:
1. Write

    $$ z=\frac{1+i\sin\theta}{1-i\sin\theta}. $$

  2. Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

    $$ z =\frac{(1+i\sin\theta)(1+i\sin\theta)}{(1-i\sin\theta)(1+i\sin\theta)} =\frac{(1+i\sin\theta)^2}{1+\sin^2\theta}. $$

  3. Expand the numerator:

    $$ (1+i\sin\theta)^2 =1+2i\sin\theta+(i\sin\theta)^2 =1+2i\sin\theta-\sin^2\theta =\cos^2\theta+2i\sin\theta. $$

  4. So

    $$ z =\frac{\cos^2\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta} +i\,\frac{2\sin\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta}. $$

  5. For $z$ to be purely imaginary, the real part must be zero:

    $$ \frac{\cos^2\theta}{1+\sin^2\theta}=0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \cos^2\theta=0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \cos\theta=0. $$

  6. $\cos\theta=0$ when

    $$ \theta=\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2},\quad n\in\mathbb{Z}. $$

Answer: $\displaystyle \theta=\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2}$ (Option B).
